title: Is the zero-divisor graph Γ(Z_17 × Z_25) prime?
generalization_signal: The four-class Γ(Z_p × Z_25) line appears to survive the first post-13 stress test: at p = 17 the refined C-block can use four below-half exception primes 37, 41, 43, 47, with their doubles pushed into D, while the old sparse barrier set B = {1,19,23,29} still has spill size only 9 and fits inside A.
strongest_honest_claim: An explicit prime-labeling witness for Γ(Z_17 × Z_25) survives skeptical verification, and the instance shows that the active four-class Γ(Z_p × Z_25) program can absorb four below-half C-block primes at p = 17 while keeping the same sparse barrier set on B. That is strong feeder evidence for a future small-spill theorem slice, but it is still only instance-level evidence rather than a proved parameterized theorem.
paper_title_hint: Verified p = 17 evidence for a small-spill C-block theorem on Γ(Z_p × Z_25)
theorem_slice_target: A quantified four-class F25 spill lemma: for odd prime p, choose p - 1 prime labels for C while allowing a bounded below-half spill whose nontrivial multiples avoid A ∪ B, together with a four-label sparse barrier set on B whose spill still fits inside the 20 A-slots.
fallback_target: Preserve Γ(Z_17 × Z_25) as a verified feeder showing that the old upper-half-prime-only C template is too rigid; if the quantified spill lemma stalls, use p = 17 and p = 19 to isolate the smallest correct hypothesis set or the first true breakpoint.
next_blocker: No quantified family lemma yet explains how many below-half C primes can be used, and the four-class F25 ring-to-support bridge layer is still incomplete.
next_action: promote the verified p = 17 four-exception pattern into the family dossier, then run z19-z25 while prioritizing the missing F25 bridge and quantified spill lemmas over exact-instance Lean
